title: Databases
text: Databases Uniface recognizes two kinds of database management systems—those that can manage individual fields within records (field-level DBMS) and those that manage records or files (record-level DBMS). Field-Level Databases A field-level DBMS stores fields as columns within a table, and maintains information about the field length and storage format of each field. Examples of field-level DBMSs are Sybase, Oracle, and Informix. Most field-level DBMSs support both a DDL and a DML. The DDL allows database objects to be defined or described. The DML, which is sometimes called a query language, allows database objects to be manipulated or processed. For many field-level DBMSs, the DML is SQL, so the term SQL-like DBMS is sometimes used to describe a field-level DBMS. Most, but not all, field-level DBMSs are relational. A Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) stores and retrieves data according to the relational data model. Not every field-level DBMS is relational, but every RDBMS is a field-level database. Record-Level Databases A record-level DBMS is a relatively simple type of DBMS that stores and retrieves data in the form of records. With the possible exception of fields within indexes, it does not support the concept of fields. Access techniques are based on the physical data structures. Records can be retrieved by their position in the file and by indexes. The majority of record-level DBMSs do not support a DDL or a DML. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
